era_context: The Seven Years' War (1756–1763) is in its second year, with the conflict expanding across multiple continents: Europe, North America, the Caribbean, and India. In Europe, Prussia under Frederick II faces pressure from Russia, Austria, and France, but achieves a major victory at Rossbach. In India, the British East India Company defeats the Nawab of Bengal at Plassey, establishing British dominance on the subcontinent. The Enlightenment is in full swing across Europe.

events.title: Battle of Plassey
events.description: British forces under Robert Clive defeat the Nawab of Bengal, Siraj-ud-Daulah, in a decisive battle near Calcutta. The victory is achieved through a combination of military tactics, diplomatic maneuvering, and the defection of key Bengali military commanders (particularly Mir Jafar). The battle is a turning point in British imperial expansion in India and marks the beginning of British territorial dominance on the subcontinent. Clive's victory establishes the British East India Company as a major political and military power in India, setting the stage for British rule over India for the next two centuries.
key_figures: Mir Jafar (Bengali general, defected to British)
Mir Madan (Bengali general, killed in battle)

sources.name: British East India Company records and Clive's own accounts
Bengali and Persian chronicles (e.g., Seir Mutaqherin)

events.certainty_note: The battle date (June 23, 1757) and basic facts are well-attested in multiple contemporary sources. The exact role of treachery and the significance of Mir Jafar's defection are debated by modern historians.
cross_references: Leads to British consolidation in Bengal (1758) and expansion across India

events.title: Battle of Rossbach
events.description: Prussian forces under Frederick II (Frederick the Great) achieve a decisive victory over a much larger combined force of Austrians and Russians near the town of Rossbach in Saxony. The battle is a masterpiece of Prussian military tactics, with Frederick using superior maneuver and concentration of force to defeat an enemy that outnumbers him significantly. The victory is a major boost to Prussian morale and demonstrates Frederick's military genius. However, despite this victory, Prussia continues to face pressure from multiple enemies and the war will continue for several more years.
key_figures: Frederick II (Frederick the Great, Prussian king)

sources.name: Prussian military records and Frederick's own writings

events.certainty_note: The battle date (November 5, 1757) and outcome are well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.
cross_references: Demonstrates Prussian military superiority despite numerical disadvantage

events.title: Battle of Kolin
events.description: Austrian forces under General Leopold von Daun defeat Prussian forces under Frederick II in a major battle near the town of Kolin in Bohemia. The battle is one of the few significant Prussian defeats of the Seven Years' War and demonstrates that Frederick is not invincible. The Austrian victory is achieved through disciplined defensive tactics and effective use of terrain. However, the Austrians are unable to follow up on their victory, and Prussia survives the war.
key_figures: Frederick II (Frederick the Great, Prussian king)

sources.name: Prussian military records and Frederick's own writings

events.certainty_note: The battle date (June 18, 1757) and outcome are well-documented in multiple contemporary sources.
cross_references: Demonstrates vulnerability of Prussian forces despite Frederick's tactical genius

events.title: Continued Seven Years' War: British naval dominance and colonial expansion
events.description: British naval forces continue to dominate global sea lanes during the Seven Years' War. British ships capture French merchant vessels and colonial possessions, disrupting French trade and colonial supply lines. Naval dominance is a key factor in British success in the war and will establish Britain as the world's leading naval power for the next century. British colonial forces also continue operations in North America, the Caribbean, and India.
key_figures: General Jeffrey Amherst (British commander-in-chief, North America)
events.certainty_note: British naval operations are extensively documented in Admiralty records.
cross_references: Establishes British naval supremacy for the 18th and 19th centuries

events.title: Publication of Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality
events.description: Jean-Jacques Rousseau publishes his Discourse on Inequality (Discours sur l'inégalité), a philosophical work that critiques the origins of human inequality and the corrupting effects of civilization. The work is influential in Enlightenment thought and challenges conventional assumptions about human nature and society. Rousseau's ideas about the 'noble savage' and the corrupting effects of private property will influence political and social thought for centuries.
events.certainty_note: The publication date of the Discourse on Inequality is 1755, not 1757. However, it was widely read and discussed in 1757. The work is included here for its influence on 1757 intellectual discourse.
cross_references: Part of broader Enlightenment intellectual movement
Influences European thought and leads to French Revolution

events.title: Expulsion of the Jesuits from France (preliminary actions)
events.description: Following the Damiens affair (an assassination attempt on King Louis XV in 1757), anti-Jesuit sentiment in France intensifies. The Jesuits are blamed for allegedly promoting regicide through their moral theology. While the formal expulsion of the Jesuits from France does not occur until 1764, the groundwork is laid in 1757 with parliamentary actions and public campaigns against the Society of Jesus. This is part of a broader Enlightenment-era campaign against Jesuit influence across Catholic Europe.
key_figures: Marquês de Pombal (Portuguese minister, parallel actions in Portugal)
events.certainty_note: Anti-Jesuit sentiment intensified in 1757 following the Damiens affair, but the formal expulsion occurred in 1764. The events of 1757 are preliminary actions.
cross_references: Leads to expulsion of Jesuits from France (1764)
Part of broader 18th-century anti-Jesuit movements across Catholic Europe

events.title: Continued Seven Years' War: French losses in North America
events.description: French forces in North America continue to lose ground to British forces. While the French achieve some tactical victories, the overall trend is toward British dominance. French supply lines are disrupted by British naval power, and French colonial forces are increasingly isolated. The year 1757 marks the beginning of the end for French military power in North America, though major battles and sieges will continue for several more years.
key_figures: General Louis-Joseph de Montcalm (French commander-in-chief, North America)
General Jeffrey Amherst (British commander-in-chief, North America)
events.certainty_note: The overall pattern of French military decline in North America in 1757 is well-documented.
cross_references: Leads to British dominance in North America by 1763

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Plassey is often portrayed as a clear-cut British military victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the battle was decided more by diplomacy, treachery, and the defection of key Bengali commanders (particularly Mir Jafar) than by superior British military tactics or force. The actual battle itself was relatively brief and one-sided, but the outcome was determined before the battle began through Clive's diplomatic maneuvering. Additionally, some accounts exaggerate the size of the British force and the Nawab's forces; modern historians have revised these estimates. The narrative of Clive as a military genius should be balanced against the recognition that his success was largely due to exploiting internal divisions within the Bengali court. The Battle of Rossbach is sometimes presented as a masterpiece of Prussian tactics, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the victory was also due to the poor coordination of the Austrian and Russian forces and the superior quality of Prussian troops.
historiographic_note: 1757 is exceptionally well-documented in British, French, Prussian, Austrian, and Russian sources due to the Seven Years' War. Military operations are extensively documented in official records, dispatches, and contemporary accounts. The Battle of Plassey is documented in British East India Company records and Bengali/Persian chronicles, though the sources reflect different perspectives and biases. British sources tend to emphasize Clive's military genius, while Bengali sources emphasize treachery and internal divisions. Cultural events (publication of Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality) are well-recorded in European sources. The historical record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; economic and social history is less extensively documented. Indigenous and non-European perspectives are underrepresented in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Battle of Plassey leads to British consolidation in Bengal (1758)
Both events are part of British expansion in India
Battles of Rossbach and Kolin are part of same campaign season
Battle of Rossbach precedes Battle of Zorndorf (1758)
Anti-Jesuit sentiment in 1757 leads to expulsion of Jesuits from Portugal (1761)
